coredata.dc:title: Histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot-graphene micro-aerogel based voltammetric sensing of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiyi, Li
Sili, Qing
Zhangyi, Li
Ling, Liu
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQDs) made via thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and histidine are combined with Zn2+ ions to form Zn-His-GQD complex. The complex was employed as the solid surfactant for stabilizing toluene-in-graphene oxide aqueous dispersion emulsion. The graphene oxide in the Pickering emulsion was slowly reduced by addition of hydrazine hydrate and leads to the formation of graphene micro-gel. The micro-gel was treated by freeze-drying, acid washing and thermal annealing to obtain His-GQD-graphene micro-aerogel (His-GQD-GMA). The as-prepared hybrid offers a three-dimensional architecture with well-defined porous structures, in which the His-GQDs were well dispersed and fixed on the graphene sheets. The all-carbon hybrid provides excellent electron/ion conductivity, electro-catalytic activity and structural stability. The sensor based on the His-GQD-GMA exhibits ultrasensitive electrochemical response to dopamine. Its differential pulse voltammetric signal linearly increases with the increase of dopamine concentration in the range from 1.0×10−9 M to 8.0×10−5 M with the detection limit of 2.9×10−10 M (at a S/N ratio of 3). The sensitivity is much better than that of the reported graphene sensors for dopamine. The analytical method has been successfully applied in the detection of dopamine in rat brain. The study also opens a window on the electronic properties of graphene aerogel and graphene quantum dots as well their hybrids to meet needs of further applications in sensors, catalysis and energy storage and conversion devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synthesis
Hybrid
Soft template method
Pickering emulsion
Differential pulse voltammetry

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of dopamine in rat brain

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The procedure for synthesis of His-GQD-GMA.
SEM image (A), enlarged SEM image (B), FTIR spectrum (C) and total XPS spectra of His-GQD-GMA.
Raman spectra of His-GQD-GMA (up) and graphene aerogel (down).
CV curves of bare GCE (a) and sensors based on the graphene (b), GA (c), GMA (d) and His-GQD-GMA (e) in a pH 7.0 PBS containing 1.0mM K4Fe(CN)6 at the scan rate of 100mVs−1.

A: CAV curves of His-GQD-GMA sensor in 1mM of K4Fe(CN)6 in pH 7.0 PBS at scan rates of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 and 100mVs−1 (from internal to external). B: Plot of the Ip vs. square root of scan rate.

AC impedance spectra of the sensors based on the common graphene (a), GA (b), GMA (c) and His-GQD-GMA (d) in a pH 7.0 PBS containing 1.0mM K4Fe(CN)6 with the equivalent circuit from the EIS measurements (inset).
CV (A) and DPV curves (B) of the His-GQD-GMA sensor in the pH 7 PBS in the absence (a) and the presence of 1×10−4 M of dopamine (b) at the scan rate of 100mVs−1.

A: DAPV curves of the in the presence of sensor in the presence of 1×10−5 M of dopamine in different pH from 5 to 8 at scan rate of 100mVs−1 (from left to right). B: Plot of the peak potentials vs. pH.
Impedance parameters calculated from equivalent circuits.
Electrochemical performances of dopamine sensors based on different sensing materials.

row.entry: Polyamide 6/poly(allylamine hydrochloride) nanofibers functionalized with multiwalled carbon nanotubes
Graphene coated by polydopamine/multi-walled carbon nanotubes
Co3O4 nanograin-decorated reduced graphene oxide composite
Graphite sheets assisted with gelatin polypeptide composite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of dopamine in rat brain samples by the proposed method and LC–MS/MS (n=5).

row.entry: The results obtained by proposed method (ngg−1)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
Results expressed as:
X
±
st
n
(n=5), where X is the mean of n observations of x, s is the standard deviation, t is distribution value chosen for the desired confidence level, the t- and F-values refer to comparison of the proposed method with the hexokinase method. Theoretical value at 95% confidence limits: F=6.39, t=2.78.

head.ce:title: Histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot-graphene micro-aerogel based voltammetric sensing of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The article reports the synthesis of Histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot-graphene micro-aerogel. The biosensor based on the hybrid exhibits the advantage of sensitivity, reproducibility and stability. The study also opens a window on the electronic properties of graphene aerogel and graphene quantum dots as well their hybrids to meet needs of further applications as the nanoelectronics in sensors, catalysis and energy storage and conversion devices.

Histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (His-GQDs) made via thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and histidine are combined with Zn2+ ions to form Zn-His-GQD complex. The complex was employed as the solid surfactant for stabilizing toluene-in-graphene oxide aqueous dispersion emulsion. The graphene oxide in the Pickering emulsion was slowly reduced by addition of hydrazine hydrate and leads to the formation of graphene micro-gel. The micro-gel was treated by freeze-drying, acid washing and thermal annealing to obtain His-GQD-graphene micro-aerogel (His-GQD-GMA). The as-prepared hybrid offers a three-dimensional architecture with well-defined porous structures, in which the His-GQDs were well dispersed and fixed on the graphene sheets. The all-carbon hybrid provides excellent electron/ion conductivity, electro-catalytic activity and structural stability. The sensor based on the His-GQD-GMA exhibits ultrasensitive electrochemical response to dopamine. Its differential pulse voltammetric signal linearly increases with the increase of dopamine concentration in the range from 1.0×10−9 M to 8.0×10−5 M with the detection limit of 2.9×10−10 M (at a S/N ratio of 3). The sensitivity is much better than that of the reported graphene sensors for dopamine. The analytical method has been successfully applied in the detection of dopamine in rat brain. The study also opens a window on the electronic properties of graphene aerogel and graphene quantum dots as well their hybrids to meet needs of further applications in sensors, catalysis and energy storage and conversion devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dot (GQD) is a zero-dimensional material with lateral size less than 100nm and consists of a single layer or few-layers of carbon atoms in a closely packed honeycomb structure [1]. GQD is expected to have significant properties owing to quantum confinement and edge effect, making it an excellent candidate for construction of nanoscaled optical and electronic devices [2]. Hence, GQDs have a variety of applications in biological imaging [3], optical sensor [4], light-emitting diode [5], optoelectronic [6] and photovoltaic [7]. Recently, GQDs as an electrode material has aroused great interest [8]. Muthurasu and Ganesh used horseradish peroxidase-functionalized GQDs as a sensing material for fabrication of biosensor. The biosensor has a high sensitivity for electrochemical detection of hydrogen peroxide [9]. However, GQD itself is one semiconductor material with poor electronic conductivity. The electrical characteristic limits its many applications in electrochemistry.

Great effort has been devoted to improve the electrochemical properties of GQDs [10]. Main ways for the improvement are doping, functionalization and hybridization. The doping is to introduce heteroatoms into hexagonal carbon structure of graphene sheets to make the heteroatom-doped GQDs. A large number of researches have demonstrated that the introduction of heteroatoms can effectively tune the intrinsic properties of GQDs, including the electronic characteristics and surface and local chemical features, which offer more active sites and induce new properties of GQDs. Now, nitrogen- [11], boron- [12], chlorine- [13], phosphorus- [14] and nitrogen and sulfur-co-doped GQDs [15] have been designed, synthesized and applied in various optical and electronic devices. The functionalization is to introduce some special functional groups into the edge of graphene sheets to fabricate the functionalized GQDs. The investigations show that the introduction of functional groups may drastically alter the electronic characteristic of GQDs, leading to unusual properties and related applications [16]. Recently, our group developed a series of the functionalized GQDs via thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and natural amino acid [17]. Among these, phenylalanine- [18], histidine- [19] and tryptophan-functionalized GQDs [20] have been applied in the modification of silicon anode for lithium ion batteries. These studies prove that the introduction of GQDs accelerate the electron transfer and electrolyte transport, leading to an obviously enhanced cell performance. Interestingly, we find that the GQDs functionalized by the amino acids containing aromatic rings often results in a much better electrochemical properties for the modification of silicon anode compared with the GQDs functionalized by the amino acids without aromatic rings. On the one hand, the introduction of aromatic structure increases the electron conjugation range, thus leading to an enhanced electronic conductivity. On the other hand, the introduction of aromatic structure causes a significant steric congestion that interferes with the self-aggregation through p–p stacking interactions. Thus, the bending, twisting and folding of GQD surfaces are most likely prevented. Moreover, the steric congestion creates the rich of channels for the electrolyte transport. This will further improves the electrochemical performance. Shadjou et al. reported a β-CD-GQD made by introducing β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) into GQD nano-structure [21]. The β-CD-GQD was studied as a new electrocatalytical composite for the electrooxidation of Vitamin C. The investigation shows that the use of β-CD-GQD can remarkably enhance the electro-activity to the oxidation of Vitamin C. However, the doped- and functionalized-GQDs still belong to semiconductor materials. Their electronic conductivities are far from carbon nanotubes and regular graphene materials. The problem can be well solved by hybrid of GQDs and noble metal nanoparticles or conducting carbon materials such as carbon nanotube and graphene. Herein, the GQD-noble metal hybrids, including GQD-Au [22], GQD-Pd [23] and Ru-GQD [24], have become a hot research topic in material science. The hybrid may be an effective strategy to enhance the functionality of materials, and the integration of nanomaterials on graphene sheets paves a new way to improve their electronic, chemical and electrochemical properties. Because of the electrostatic repulsion between negatively charged GQDs and negatively charged noble metal nanoparticles, GQDs are difficult to full contact with noble metal nanoparticles. In addition, the GQD-noble metal hybrids also lack of a stable three-dimensional architecture with well-defined porous structures. These factors limit the ion transport between electrode and electrolyte, leading to a poor electrochemical performance. More recently, Wang et al. reported an all-carbon hybrid electrocatalyst for the metal/air batteries [25]. The hybrid offers a three-dimensional architecture with the rich of porous structures. Its GQDs nested in the graphene aerogel offer more catalytically active sites, while the graphene hydrogel acts as a conductive substrate to fix and sputter GQDs to avert the agglomeration of GQDs and facilitate the electron transfer. The pure carbon material exhibits an excellent electro-catalytic activity and nice durability in alkaline solution. However, the pure carbon material has to be crushed into tiny powder before its use, because the sensor preparation mostly requires the use of tiny powder material rather than large size of graphene aerogel. The tearing will seriously destroy the characteristic structure of graphene hydrogel and lead to partial loss of the excellent electronic and mechanical performances of the pristine graphene aerogel. In addition, the resulted graphene powder is still difficult to be well dispersed in common solvents to form a stable dispersion due to its relatively large size. The above problems not only bring inconvenience of the sensor preparation but also leads to great decline in the electro-catalytic activity. The hybrid of functionalized GQDs and graphene micro-aerogel that offers an excellent electrocatalytic activity and can be directly dispersed in a solvent for the sensor preparation is highly anticipated.

The study focuses on synthesis of histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot-graphene micro-aerogel (His-GQD-GMA) using a Pickering emulsion as the soft template. The as-prepared all-carbon hybrid offers a three-dimensional architecture structures with high porosity. Its His-GQDs were well dispersed and fixed on the graphene sheets. The sensor based on the His-GQD-GMA exhibits an ultrahigh sensitivity for electrochemical detection of dopamine.

Dopamine, citric acid and histidine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company (Mainland, China) (http://www.sigmaaldrich.com/china-mainland.html). Graphite oxide (GO) was prepared from natural graphite (particle size: 7000mesh) using the Hummers’ method [26]. The His-GQD and Zn-His-GQD were synthesized using the reported methods [27]. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.0, Na2HPO4-KH2PO4-NaCl-KCl, 0.01M) was prepared in the laboratory. All other reagents employed were of analytical reagent grade or with the highest quality and were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.,Ltd (Shanghai, China) (http://www.reagent.com.cn). Ultra pure water (18.2MΩcm) purified from a Milli-Q purification system was used throughout the experiment.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) analysis was carried out in HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. SEM sample was prepared by placing a drop of dilute ethanol dispersion of the composites onto a copper plate attached to an aluminum sample holder, and the solvent was allowed to evaporate at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed with PHI 5700 ESCA spectrometer with monochromated Al KR radiation (hν =1486.6eV). Raman measurement was carried out using a InVia laser micro-Raman spectrometer. Infrared spectrum (IR) was recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer. The N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms were measured at 77K on the Quantachrome Nova 2000. Prior to the gas sorption measurement, all the samples were outgassed in vacuum at 120°C for 24h. The specific surface area and the pore size distribution were calculated using the Braunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method and the relative pressure range of p/p0 from 0.1 to 0.3 was used for multipoint BET calculations. The optical microscopy image of emulsion was recorded with VHX-1000C Shooting optical microscope (Kean Co. Ltd, Hongkong in China). Electronic conductivity measurement was carried out in a ST-2258C multifunction digital four-probe tester which equipped with a SZT-D semiconductor powder resistivity tester (Suzhou Jingge Electronic Co., Ltd., China). The powder sample was directly placed in a round hole with the diameter of 5mm on the SZT-D semiconductor powder resistivity tester. Then, the powder was slowly compressed by the piston on the system. At the same time, the electrical conductivity values under different pressures were automatically recorded.

Zn-His-GQD (125mg) was ultrasonically dispersed in the GO aqueous dispersion (30ml, 5mgml−1) in a beaker (100ml). Added toluene (20ml) and then stirred for 20s at 13000rpm on a HN–30K handheld homogenizer (Shanghai HANUO Instrument Co. Ltd, China) to form an oil-in-water emulsion. After added hydrazine hydrate (0.45g), the emulsion was heated at 40°C for 4h and then dried by freeze-drying. The resulted GO micro-hydrogel was immersed in a hydrochloric acid solution (0.1M, 10ml) for 5min and centrifugation at 9000rpm. The collected precipitate was washed for 2 times with ultra pure water, dried at 80°C in vacuum and heated at 350°C in N2 for 4h To obtain His-GQD-GMA.

To evaluate the influence of material structure on the electrochemical properties, three control samples, including common graphene, graphene aerogel (GA) and graphene micro-aerogel (GMA), were prepared using the same GO product. Here, the common graphene was obtained by direct reduction of GO at 350°C in N2 for 4h. The GA was made in the following the procedure: the hydrazine hydrate (0.45g) was added into the GO aqueous dispersion (30ml, 5mgml−1). The dispersion was then heated at 40°C until the GO hydrogel is formed. Then, it was treated by freeze-drying and thermal annealing at 350°C in N2 for 4h to obtain GA solid sample. The GMA was fabricated by using the same procedure for the synthesis of His-GQD-GMA except for addition of one alkali washing step to remove the His-GQDs from the surface of GO micro-aerogel between the acid washing and the water washing. The alkali washing was carried out for 3 times using an aqueous sodium hydroxide solution (0.1M, 30ml).

Electrochemical experiments were performed with a CHI660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai, China). Conventional three electrode system was used for cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement with Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) electrode as the reference electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode, and bare or modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 1.0mm in diameter) as the working electrode. Before use, GCE was polished successively with 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05μm alumina powder, and sonicated in a 6.0M of nitric acid/doubly distilled water and ethanol/doubly distilled water for 20min. Then, the GCE as working electrode was subjected to cyclic scanning in 0.5M of sulphuric acid solution in a potential range from −0.1V to 1.0V. When the cyclic voltammogram was almost unchanged, the electrode was taken out, cleaned with ultra pure water and dried in a stream of nitrogen.

The sensor preparations were carried out using the following procedure: His-GQD-GMA sample (or other sensing materials) was dispersed in ethanol to make up 1.0mgml−1 dispersion, then 5μl of the dispersion was dropped on the surface of GCE. After that, 2μl of the chitosan solution was carefully spreading on the His-GQD-GMA surface of GCE and dried in air before use. The electrochemical experiment was carried out under room temperature, and all experimental solution was degassed by nitrogen for at least 15min. Then, a nitrogen atmosphere was maintained during electrochemical measurements. DPV parameters were set to a scan rate of 4mVs−1, 50mV pulse amplitude, 20ms pulse width and −0.2V initial potential. For the EIS measurement a potential amplitude of ±5mV and a frequency range of 0.01–105 Hz were adopted.

The His-GQD-GMA synthesis includes three assembly processes (shown in Fig. 1 ). Firstly, the Zn-His-GQD, GO aqueous dispersion (water phase) and toluene (oil phase) were mixed and strongly stirred to still form a stable Pickering emulsion. In the process, the Zn-His-GQD was used as a solid particle surfactant for stabilizing toluene-in GO aqueous dispersion emulsion. As Zn-His-GQD has an appropriate amphiphilic structure, its surface activity is much better than that of the pristine His-GQD. Its essembly on the oil/water interface largely reduces total interfacial energy that occurs when the part of the liquid/liquid interface is replaced with the liquid/particle interface. The study demonstrates that the Pickering emulsion stabilized by Zn-His-GQD is of high emulsion stability. As Zn-His-GQD offers a relatively strong hydrophilicity and a relatively weak hydrophobicity, the formed emulsion is oil in water. Accordingly, water is the continuous phase while the oil is the dispersed phase in the emulsion system. Because of high water-solubility, GO sheets mainly exist in aqueous phase. Further, optical microscopic image of the Pickering emulsion in Fig. S1 reveals that the formed oil droplets are regular microspheres with a wide size distribution.

The amounts of hydrazine hydrate was added into the above Pickering emulsion. To obtain GO micro-hydrogel, the emulsion was heated to 40°C in a water bath and kept the temperature for 4h. In the process, the GO sheets in the aqueous phase will be partly reduced by hydrazine hydrate. The reduction leads to the decrease in the hydrophilicity and the increase in the hydrophobicity due to the loss of hydrophilic groups. Driven by water molecules, the partly reduced GO sheets enters into the interior of the oil droplets. These hydrophobic reduced graphene sheets in the oil droplets strongly tend to assemble into the three-dimensional macroscopic structure through p-p interactions, finally resulting in the formation of GO micro-gels. As the reaction temperature is strictly controlled at 40°C, velocity of the graphene sheets that reach to the interior of oil droplets is slow and remains almost constant. The slow and constant process leave enough time for the self-assembly and ordered arrangement of GO sheets. As a result, the resulted GO micro-aerogel are composed of dense and fine graphene framework networks. This greatly improves the electronic conductivity and mechanical strength of the final product.

The GO micro-hydrogel was treated by freeze-drying, acid washing and thermal annealing in N2 to obtain the His-GQD-GMA. In the process, thermal annealing was empolyed for the further reduction of GO sheets. The chemically active groups at the edge of graphene sheets will be fully stripped by the thermal annealing and leads to an improved electronic conductivity. However, the use of too high temperature for the thermal annealing may lead to the loss of functional groups in the His-GQD and remarkably reduces the catalytic active sites. For the above reason, a moderate temperature, 350°C, was selected for the thermal annealing.

The as-prepared His-GQD-GMA was characterized by SEM, FTIR, XPS and Raman spectrum techologies. As shown in Fig. 2 , His-GQD-GMA is composed of graphene micro-aerogels with the particle size range from 5 to 16μM. The small size of His-GQD-GMA can be directly dispersed in ethanol to form a stable dispersion (see Fig. S2). Thus, further grinding of the His-GQD-GMA sample is not needed before use for the sensor preparation. This not only brings the convenience and high reproducibility of the batch production but also can maintain the integrity of the original graphene aerogel structure. The structural integrity palys an important role in mantaining excellent electrochemical properties. The enlarge SEM image further reveals that each GMA of the His-GQD-GMA is made up of dense and fine graphene framework networks. The fine construction significantly improves the electronic conductivity of graphene. To verify the suggest, the electronic conductivities of different graphene materials were measured using a four-probe method. The result shows that the common graphene offers the lowest electronic conductivity (986.3Sm−1) among four kinds of graphene materials. As the common graphene is constructed by physically and randomly stacked graphene sheets, the electronic conduction between graphene sheets is partly blocked due to a relatively big distance and leads to a poor electronic conductivity. The constituent graphene sheets in the graphene aerogel materials, including GA, GMA and His-GQD-GMA, are chemically bonded, facilitating much faster charge transport across graphene sheet junctions, indicating a high electronic conductivity. Among three graphene aerogel samples, GA has the lowest electronic conductivity (1584.8Sm−1). This could be attributed to their difference in the synthesis method. Compared with the synthesis of GA, the preparation of His-GQD-GMA and GMA adopted a slow and constant reduction process of the GO sheets. The slow and constant process leaves enough time for self-assembly and ordered arrangement of GO sheets, the formed graphene framework networks are denser and finer. This can greatly improve the electronic conductivity of graphene aerogel. Althrough His-GQD-GMA and GMA have an almost same structure, the electronic conductivity of His-GQD-GMA (2750.2Sm−1) is better than that of GMA (2150.7Sm−1). The increased electronic conductivity could be attributed to introduction of His-GQD.

There are many IR absorption peaks and bands on the FTIR spectrum of His-GQD-GMA. Since chemically active groups on the GO sheets have been fully stripped during the thermal annealing, the IR absorptions in Fig. 2C are mainly attributed to IR absorption of the His-GQD. The peak at 3425cm−1 is the symmetric stretching vibration IR absorption of NH2. The peak at 1697cm−1 can be assigned to the CO stretch vibration. The peak at 1387cm−1 is attributed to the CH stretching vibration, suggesting the presence of carbonyl and CH group. The band at between 3600cm−1 and 3300cm−1 is correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. Fig. 2C also shows that the FTIR spectrum exists several IR absorption bands at between 2000cm−1 and 500cm−1. Almost all characteristics IR absorption peaks of histidine can be found from these bands, including NH2, COOH and imidazole groups. On the total XPS spectrum, there are three main XPS peaks at about 284, 400 and 531eV, corresponding to carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements. The result confirms that the His-GQD-GMA is composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. Because nitrogen and oxygen elements mainly come from the His-GQD, the above result also demonstrates that the His-GQD has been successfully coated on the graphene sheets during the synthesis. On the C1s XPS spectrum in Fig. S3 there are three XPS peaks. The peaks located at 284.4, 286.6 and 288.1eV can be assigned to CC, CO and CO species, respectively. On the N1s XPS spectrum in Fig. S3 there are three XPS peaks. The peak at 398.4eV can be assigned to N2, corresponds to pyridinic N. The peak at 399.8eV can be assigned to pyrrolic N and amine. The peak at 401eV can be assigned to N4 and corresponds to graphitic N, indicating that nitrogen replaced the carbon in the graphene sheets and was incorporated into the carbon network. The result of FTIR and XPS analysis verifies that the hybrid of His-GQD and GM was achieved.

Fig. 3 presents Raman spectra of GMA and His-GQD-GMA. Raman spectrum of GMA displays two distinct peaks at around 1350 and 1612cm−1, corresponding to D peak and G peak of the carbon material. The D band could attribute to the A1g vibration mode of carbon atoms with double-resonance process in plane terminations of disordered graphite. The G band arises from the E2g mode of graphitic carbon and is assigned to the vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the graphite layer. However, two characteristic Raman peaks of the His-GQD-GMA shifted to 1386 and 1606cm−1. The G band energy remained virtually unchanged, whereas the D band energy showed a substantial blue-shift of 35cm−1. The fact verifies that the energy transfer between the His-GQD and the GMA was achieved. The behavior analogous to hole doping that has been found to lead to an increase of the D band frequency of graphene [28].

To evaluate the electrocatalytic activity of His-GQD-GMA, four kinds of sensors based on the different graphene materials were fabricated using the same procedure. Their CV behavours were investigated in the pH 7.0 PBS in a 1.0mM Fe(CN)6 4− aqueous solution at the scan rate of 100mVs−1. Fig. 4 shows that the common graphene sensor offers a higher peak current with a smaller peak potential on the CV curve compared with the bare GCE. The result verifies that the graphene provides high electrocatalytic activity to [Fe(CN)4]3−/4−. The high electrocatalytic activity could attribute to the large specific surface and hgih electronic conductivity of common graphene. However, the CV response is lower than that of GA sensor. This is because the constituent graphene sheets in the GA are chemically bonded, facilitating much faster charge transport across graphene sheet junctions, large specific surface that offers abundant active sites for the catalytic reduction events, and big pore volume that provides fast mass transfer of the redox species. The above factors further enhance the electrocatalytic activity and lead to a more sensitive CV response during the electrode reaction. But the peak current of GA sensor is lower than that of both GMA sensor and His-GQD-GMA sensor. This is because GMA and His-GQD-GMA contain a denser and finer graphene framework networks compared with the GA. The structural characteristic makes GMA and His-GQD-GMA a higher electronic conductivity, which improves the CV response of the sensor to Fe(CN)6 4−. Fig. 4 also indicates that the His-GQD-GMA sensor gives a higher peak current and a smaller peak potential on the CV curve compared with the GMA sensor, verifying that the His-GQD-GMA can offer a better electrocatalytic activity. The GMA and His-GQD-GMA have an almost same structure, the improvement could attribute to His-GQD. For the His-GQD, its graphene sheets exist many imidazole groups at the edge. Its introduction increases the range of electron delocalization via the π-π conjugation between imidazole and graphene sheet and results in a higher electronic conductivity. This will further improve the CV response of the sensor.

To research on dynamic behavior of the electrode reaction, CV curves of the His-GQD-GMA sensor in the pH 7.0 PBS containing 1.0mM Fe(CN)6 4− were measured at different scan rates. Fig. 5 shows that the peak current on the CV curves rapidly increases with the increase of scan rate in the range of 10–100mVs−1, but the peak potential is nearly independent of the scan rate. The result demonstrates that the His-GQD-GMA mediator was efficiently connected on the GCE surface for facile charge transfer. Both cathodic and anodic peak currents are linearly proportional with the square root of scan rate, verifying that the electrode reaction corresponds to the solution phase is a quasi-reversible electrochemical process. In addition, the electrochemical stability of His-GQD-GMA sensor was checked by a repetitive potential sweep at a scan rate of 200mVs−1. The result shows that the peak current maintains the same original intensity after 100 scans. This verifies that the electrochemistry of the sensor is very stable and the leakage of His-GQD-GMA form the sensor surface can be neglected.

Electrochemical impedance behavior of the sensors based on the different graphene materials in the pH 7.0 PBS containing 1.0mMK4Fe(CN)6 was studied to further study on the kinetic process of electrode reaction. Fig. 6 shows that Nyquist spectrum of the each sensor includes one semicircle in high frequency and a subsequent inclined line at the low frequency end. The semicircle in high frequency region is related to the ionic conduction through the electrolyte and electronic conduction between the substrate and the active material. The sloping straight line at low frequency is corresponded to the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− diffusion in the bulk materials. The pattern of EIS can be fitted by the equivalent circuit shown in the inset of Fig. 6. In the equivalent circuit, Rs is the bulk electrolyte resistance, Rct is the electron and charge transfer resistance, Ci is the interfacial capacitance, and Zw is Warburg resistance related to a combination of the diffusional effects of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− ion on the interface between the active material particles and electrolyte, which is generally indicated by a straight sloping line at low frequency end. In general, the combination of Rct and Zw is called faradic impedance, which reflects kinetics of the electrode reaction, and a low Rct corresponds to a fast kinetics of the faradic reaction. Table 1 shows that the Rct of GA sensor is much lower than that of the common graphene sensor, indicating a faster electrode reaction. The result demonstrates that the GA offers a better electro-catalytic activity compared with the common graphene. The Rct will further reduce when the GA was replaced by the GMA and the His-GQD-GMA. This is mainly attributed to a higher electronic conductivity of GMA and His-GQD-GMA. Compared with GMA sensor, His-GQD-GMA sensor exhibits a lower Rct value, indicating a faster electrode reaction. The result verifies that the introduction of His-GQD further improves the electro-catalytic activity.

Based on the results of electrochemical impedance measurement, the diffusion coefficients of Fe(CN)6 4− (D) in the modified layer were calculated by the Eq. (1) [29]:
(1)
D =
R
2
T
2
2
A
2
n
4
F
4
C
2
σ
2
where R is the gas constant, T is the temperature, n is the number of electrons transferred, A is the surface area of the electrode, F is the faraday constant, and C is the concentration of Fe(CN)6 4−. The value of parameter σ is the slope of real part of impedance (Zre) versus inverse square root of radial frequency (ω−1/2) in the Warburg region. The D value for different sensor was calculated by using the Eq. (1) and the result was listed in Table 1. Table 1 shows that the common graphene sensor has the lowest diffusion coefficient among four sensors. This is mainly attributed to its large size of graphene sheets. The micron-sized graphene sheets in the common graphene seriously block the electrolyte transport in the modified layer on the electrode surface during the electrode reaction, leading to a slow diffusion process. The diffusion coefficient will increase to more than 28 times if the common graphene was replaced by GA. This is attributed to the three-dimensional structure of GA. The structure offers the rich of open pores and a much larger specific surface area (1224.3m2 g−1) compared with the common graphene (672.9m2 g−1). The large pore volume provides a fast mass transfer of Fe(CN)6 3−/4−, and the large surface area provides abundant active sites for the catalytic reduction events. These factors help to improve the kinetic process of electrode reaction, improving the diffusion process of Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. However, the diffusion coefficient of GMA sensor is more than 16 times that of GA sensor. This is contributed to larger specific surface and higher structural stability of GMA. The specific surface area of GMA reaches to 1517.1m2 g−1. Since the value is bigger than that of GA, the GMA can provide more abundant active sites for the catalytic reduction events, further increasing chances of the contact between the GMA and the Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. The high structural stability of GMA will greatly reduce the loss of the specific surface area and the channel blockage caused by the collapse of porous structures during the sensor preparation and the electrode reaction. The more larger surface area and structural stability result in a faster kinetic process of electrode reaction, accelerating the diffusion process of Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. Table 1 also shows that the diffusion coefficient of His-GQD-GMA sensor is more than 3 times that of GMA sensor. The His-GQD-GMA has a similar specific surface area (1590.3m2 g−1) and three-dimensional structure, thus an increased diffusion coefficient could attribute to His-GQD. The His-GQD contains the rich of active groups that are easier to combine with Fe(CN)6 3−/4− ions to realize a faster electrolyte transport during the electrode reaction. On the other hand, spongy His-GQD layer on the graphene sheets works as an ion reservoir to contain a host of the electrolyte, shortening the transport path of Fe(CN)6 3−/4− ions. For the above reasons, the introduction of His-GQD speeds up the electrode reaction and results in a faster diffusion process of Fe(CN)6 3−/4−.

To study on the electrochemical response of His-GQD-GMA sensor towards dopamine, its CV and DPV habeviours in the pH 7.0 PBS were investigated in the absence and the presence of dopamine. Fig. 7 A shows that no any redox peak appears on the CV curve in the absence of dopamine, indicating no existence of the redox reactions on the His-GQD-GMA electrode. The result also proves that the His-GQD-GMA itself does not participate in the electrode reaction. However, the addition of dopamine leads to appear a pair of strong redox peaks on the CV curve. The reversible redox peaks are attributed to the oxidation and reduction of dopamine on the electrode surface, in which the His-GQD-GMA only acts as an electrocatalyst to accelerate the redox reaction of dopamine. Fig. 7A also shows a small peak potential separation (ΔEp) for dopamine on the CV curve of His-GQD-GMA sensor. The ΔEp is about 0.030V that is very close to the theory value of 0.0592V/n, indicating that the oxidation and reduction of dopamine (n=2) on the electrode surface is an almost reversible process. Fig. 7B presents DPV curves of the sensor in the absence and presence of dopamine. It can be seen from Fig. 7B that the DPV curve is almost a straight line and no any DPV peak can be observed in the absence of dopamine. Thus, the reaction medium cannot interfere with the detection of dopamine using the DPV. However, the DPV curve offers a strong DPV peak at between 0.0 and 0.4V in the presence of dopamine. Because the DPV can offer a better current response and resolution compared with CV for dopamine, it was selected for the electrochemical detection.

DPV curves of the His-GQD-GMA sensor in the PBS of different pH containing 1.0×10−5 M dopamine were shown in Fig. 8 A. It can be seen that the peak currents and peak potentials of dopamine exist an obvious difference in the different pH on the DPV curves. The peak current increases with the increase of pH when the pH is lower than 7. This is mainly attributed to the protonation of amino groups in both dopamine and His-GQD in a relatively low pH medium. The protonation leads to the formation of positively charged dopamine and His-GQD ions. The positively charged dopamine ions are difficult to be adsorbed on the His-GQD-GMA surface due to their strong electrostatic repulsion, leading to a weak peak current on the DPV curve. The number of positively charged dopamine ions rapidly decreases with the increase of pH and free dopamine molecules increases, thus the concentration of dopamine on the electrode surface increases with the increase of pH, leading to an enhanced peak current signal. The peak current reaches to the maximum value when the pH is about 7. However, the peak current reduces with the increase of pH when the pH is more than 7. This is mainly attributed to the dissociation of the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in both dopamine and His-GQD in a weak base medium. The dissociation produces negatively charged dopamine and His-GQD ions. The negatively charged dopamine ions are difficult to be adsorbed on the His-GQD-GMA surface due to the strong electrostatic repulsion between dopamine and His-GQD. This decreases adsorption capacity of dopamine on the electrode surface produces a high overpotential, indicating a reduced peak current on the DPV curve. To obtain a high sensitivity, the PBS of pH 7.0 was selected for the electrochemical detection of dopamine.

Fig. 8B presents the relationship curves between pH and the peak potential for the anode. The equation for the peak potential with pH is Epa =−0.0593pH+0.5277 (mV, R2 =0.99) for the anode. The slopes for the anode is close to the theory value of 0.0592V/pH, verifying that the uptake of electrons is accompanied by an equal number of protons for each of the isomers. The electron transfer numbers (n) for dopamine were calculated by based on the relationship between E p and pH. The result is 2, indicating that the redox reaction of dopamine at the sensor may be a two electron and two proton process.

Fig. 9 presents the DPV curves of the sensor in the pH 7 PBS containing different concentrations of dopamine. The peak current on the DPV curve linearly increases with the increase of dopamine concentration when the concentration of dopamine is in the range of 1.0×10−9–8.0×10−5 M. The corresponding linear regression equations is: Ip =−0.5482C−9.2142, with statistically significant correlation coefficient of 0.997. Here, the Ip is the peak current in the DPV in μA and C is the concentration of dopamine in the unit of μM. The detection limit is 2.9×10−10 M for dopamine that was obtained from the signal-to-noise characteristics of these data (S/N=3). The sensitivity is much better than that of the sensor based on the GMA (1.7×10−6 M) or His-GQD (5.8×10−5 M). The result demonstrates that hybrid of GMA and His-GQD leads to an improved electro-catalytic activity to dopamine. The sensitivity also is much better than that of the reported sensors in Table 2 .

The successive detection of 1.0×10−5 M dopamine by using the same sensor and the RSD of the sensor in 30 successive detection of dopamine is 1.2%, indicating the good repeativity. To evaluate the long-term stability, His-GQD-GMA sensor was fabricated and then stored it at 4°C for four weeks. The sensor was taken out for each week to measure the DPV signal in 1.0×10−5 M of dopamine. The peak current remained 98.7% of its original DPV response at least after four weeks, indicating good long-term stability. Fifty His-GQD-GMA sensors were prepared by using the same procedure and their DPV signals in 1.0×10−5 M of dopamine were measured. For the measurements, the relative standard deviations (RSD%) of the peak currents is 1.66%, indicating good reproducibility. The good reproducibility is mainly attributed to the small size and relatively high hydrophilicity of His-GQD-GMA. The characteristics make it easy to directly disperse in ethanol to form stable dispersion. Good stability of the dispersion helps us to effectively improve the reproducibility for sensor preparation.

The anti-interference is a significant parameter of the electrochemical sensor for detection of dopamine. Main inorganic ions are Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, CI−, CO3 2−, PO4 3− and SO4 2− in biological samples. Their effect on the detection of dopamine have been investigated. The addition of 0.35mM of Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, CI−, CO3 2−, PO4 3− or SO4 2− in a 1.0×10−5 M of dopamine only causes the DPV signal change of less than 2%. The good selectivity is attributed to the electrochemical inertia of the above ions. These ions can not produce any electrochemical response by DPV under the optimum conditions for the detection due to their poor electrochemical activity. These ions also cannot react with dopamine to form stable compounds and influence on the concentration of free dopamine in the system. Thus, the existence of above ions doesn’t cause interference with the detection of dopamine. Besides these inorganic ions, urea, ascorbic acid and dopamine often coexist in many biological sample and most likely interfere with detection of dopamine due to their high electrochemical activity. To evaluate the effect of urea and ascorbic acid on detection of dopamine, DPV behaviors of the sensor in the presence of urea and ascorbic acid were also investigated. Fig. S4 shows that urea and ascorbic acid give a sensitive DPV response, but their peak potentials on the DPV curves exist in an obvious difference with that of dopamine. The further investigation shows that the introduction of 10-fold urea and ascorbic acid can only cause the changes of 1.0% and 2.7% in the DPV signal of dopamine. The results prove that the His-GQD-GMA sensor offers a high selectivity for electrochemical detection of trace dopamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of dopamine in rat brain
ce:section.ce:para: Rat brain tissue sample was homogenized in acetonitrile. After homogenizing thoroughly, the dopamine from brain tissue were further extracted by sonication for 60s. The homogenates of brain tissue were centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 10min at 4°C. The collected supernatant was used for the detection of dopamine in rat brain tissue samples by the proposed method and liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) method [41]. The mean±SD of each sample were calculated and the values are reported in Table 3 . Table 3 shows that there is a very good agreement between the results obtained by the proposed sensor and those obtained by LC–MS/MS.

The study demonstrated a promising strategy for synthesis of histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot-graphene micro-aerogel via a Pickering emulsion as the soft template. The resulted hybrid offers the three-dimensional structure with the rich of pores. Its graphene quantum dots were fully dispersed and fixed on the graphene sheets. The study reveals that the hybrid of graphene quantum dots and graphene micro-aerogel brings an enhanced electro-catalytic activity. The sensor based on the His-GQD-GMA exhibits an ultrasensitive electrochemical response to dopamine. The study also opens a window on the electronic properties of graphene aerogel and graphene quantum dots as well their hybrids to meet needs of further applications as nanoelectronics in sensors, catalysis and energy storage and conversion devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115), Prospective Joint Research Project: Cooperative Innovation Fund (No. BY2015019-26) and MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project (B13025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.05.001.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Tunable optical properties of OH-functionalised graphene quantum dots
Target delivery and cell imaging using hyaluronic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Dopamine fluorescent sensors based on polypyrrole/graphene quantum dots core/shell hybrids
Easy extraction of water-soluble graphene quantum dots for light emitting diodes
Graphene, graphene quantum dots and their applications in optoelectronics

Graphene quantum dot-doped polyaniline nanofiber as high performance supercapacitor electrode materials
Horseradish peroxidase enzyme immobilized graphene quantum dots as electrochemical biosensors
Appl. Biochem. Biotechnol. Part A. Enzyme Eng. Biotechnol.
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive, label-free detection of Fe (III) in aqueous media

Multicolour light emission from chlorine-doped graphene quantum dot
Effects of boron and phosphorus doping on the photoluminescence of self-assembled germanium quantum dots

Ultrafast synthesis of gold/proline-functionalized graphene quantum dots and its use for ultrasensitive electrochemical detection of p-acetamidophenol

Study on the synthesis and electrochemical performance of histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots@silicon composite anode material

Integration of β-cyclodextrin into graphene quantum dot nano-structure and its application towards detection of Vitamin C at physiological pH: a new electrochemical approach
A sensitive and selective electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dot/gold nanoparticle nanocomposite modified electrode for the determination of quercetin in biological samples
Oxygen electroreduction catalyzed by palladium nanoparticles supported on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots: impacts of nitrogen dopants
Intervalence charge transfer of ruthenium-nitrogen moieties embedded within nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Synergistically enhanced activity of graphene quantum dots/graphene hydrogel composites: a novel all-carbon hybrid electrocatalyst for metal/air batteries

Fabrication of zinc–histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot framework amphiphilic nanoparticles and application in the synthesis of polystyrene microspheres for adsorption of Cu2+ by Pickering emulsion polymerization
The dependence of graphene raman D-band on carrier density
Simultaneous measurements and modeling of the electrochemical impedance and the cyclic voltammetric characteristics of graphite electrodes doped with lithium
Evaluation of a new biosensor based on in-situ synthesized PPy-Ag-PVP nanohybrid for selective detection of dopamine
Carbon nanotubes grown on metal microelectrodes for the detection of dopamine
Electrospun polyamide 6/poly(allylamine hydrochloride) nanofibers functionalized with carbon nanotubes for electrochemical detection of dopamine
Graphene coated by polydopamine/multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified electrode for highly selective detection of dopamine and uric acid in the presence of ascorbic acid
Preparation of quantum dots CdTe decorated graphene composite for sensitive detection of uric acid and dopamine
One-pot facile fabrication of graphene-zinc oxide composite and its enhanced sensitivity for simultaneous electrochemical detection of ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid
A highly sensitive and stable electrochemical sensor for simultaneous detection towards ascorbic acid, dopamine, and uric acid based on the hierarchical nanoporous PtTi alloy
Facile fabrication of cobalt oxide nanograin-decorated reduced graphene oxide composite as ultrasensitive platform for dopamine detection
A facile electrochemical sensor based on well-dispersed graphene-molybdenum disulfide modified electrode for highly sensitive detection of dopamine
A novel l-leucine modified Sol-Gel-Carbon electrode for simultaneous electrochemical detection of homovanillic acid, dopamine and uric acid in neuroblastoma diagnosis
A simple preparation of graphite/gelatin composite for electrochemical detection of dopamine
Direct detection of tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) and dopamine in rat brain using liquid chromatography coupled electrospray tandem mass spectrometry

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Ruiyi is a PhD student in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. She received his master degree from The Birmingham University, The United Kingdom, 2014. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their application in electrochemical sensors.
Qing Sili is an undergraduate in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is electrochemical sensors for food safety.
Li Zhangyi is an undergraduate in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene quantum dots.

Liu Ling is a master candidate in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of graphene nanohybrids and their application in electrochemical sensors.

Li Zaijun is a professor of material chemistry in Jiangnan University, China. He received his doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, in 2004. He is an author of more than 300 papers. He also is editor of Journal of Food Safety Quality, editor-in-chief of Synthesis & catalysis: open Access and special reviewers of more than thirty international journals. His current research interests are synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in sensing, electrocatalysis and energy storage/conversion devices.
